source: Gaze Glass — A Family of Mortals (2).docx
heading: 3 Rashid
paragraphs: Marok poked his snout out from a cloud and watched seventeen-year-old Rashid Shah and his best friend, Jermaine Okoro, stand with a malnourished brown calf from a mile above.

The poor baby was found chewing on scraps of plastic left by tourists on the banks of the blue Ganges river. The boys held out pieces of banana to guide the calf up Rishikesh’s largest mountain path where parched soil was covered in a carpet of dead leaves. Rashid’s parents had instructed the boys to find and bring Sahil home. The boys hadn’t noticed the sun peeking through wet leaves as it lowered; it watched from above the mountains and snickered at the boys’ persistence. Soon, the pink sky welcomed a full moon.

Cool wind threatened to blow Rashid away, but he dug his worn sandals into the earth. Rashid’s bony shoulders held his wrinkly white short-sleeved cotton shirt like a flimsy tent and his baggy cargo shorts billowed like hot air balloons. His golden brown pointed nose sloped up to warm, honey-colored eyes. The boy wore his hair naturally long, and when he turned his head, loose, black curls moved like cobras through tall fields of grass at sunrise.

“Yo! Your mom said we needed to be home before sunset,” Jermaine said. He tucked a long, black braid behind his ear, and his ashy dark skin sipped sunlight. “What do we do about the cow? It’s not like we can take it back to the cottage? Wait. Can we?”

“I haven’t gotten that far yet,” Rashid admitted. “And probably not. We need to find Sahil before my mom loses it.” The boys strolled past unpainted cottages and food stands. Rashid avoided touching anything or anyone and eyed a boy—about the same age as Sahil—mashing potatoes against a sizzling metal grill with a wooden spoon. Jermaine sniffed the air and smiled. “Is that cumin and ginger?” he asked. “Yo! Those potatoes smell like your mom’s aloo bhaji! Did I say that right? That’s the name of that potato dish I liked. Yeah?”

Before Rashid could reply, three fat men with short black hair shoved Jermaine to the side and slapped the wooden stall. The boy mashing the potatoes flinched and dropped his spoon. “Give us eight potato sandwiches! No onion! No sauce or I’ll mess you up!” said one of the men; the two behind him smiled and cracked their knuckles. Jermaine returned to his feet, dark eyes glowering. “Yo! That wasn’t cool!” he yelled in English. The men turned at once and studied the boys. The man who barked orders spat on the ground near Jermaine’s foot and laughed. “Shut your mouth, foreigner!” Rashid flinched and bit his tongue.
“What did—” Jermaine growled. “I’m going to whoop your—.”

Rashid grabbed Jermaine’s beefy arm and pulled him away. Although Jermaine wasn’t violent by nature, Rashid knew what his best friend was capable of. After school, his sister, Priya, brought Jermaine with her and Sahil to martial arts classes. “It’s not worth it man. Let it go!” Rashid yelled. Jermaine groaned and nodded. Rashid smiled and pointed forward, urging them to move along. He bent his knees and lured the calf forward with more fruit.
“One of these days you’re going to have to fight back,” Jermaine said and clapped.
Rashid’s chin dipped to his chest, eyes rolling away.
“You know what I’m talking about. I could have taken those guys!”
“And what would that have accomplished?” Rashid asked, brows high.
“Taught them to think before messing with people!” Jermaine shouted.
“The locals would call you violent and you’d make it harder for people like you—”
Jermaine cleared his throat. “People like me?” he interrupted. “You got something to say?”

After Jermaine’s family passed away in a fire, Rashid’s family adopted him. He was consumed by grief and rage—and lashed out whenever he was provoked. Despite Rashid’s best efforts, he could not always keep the peace. “You aren’t from here! You don’t know what these people are like. They are judgmental—they are small-minded. We’re from the south and they treat us like we’re from another planet, which is ridiculous considering my family worked hard to achieve their fortune; we invested so much of it in farms and holy places—and what do they do? Leave plastic bottles and trash near the temples. They even burned down a mosque! If you choose violence, you are only making it harder for them to trust foreigners!”
Jermaine flinched. “How will they ever respect anyone if there aren’t consequences?”
“I don’t know—” Rashid started.
“Then what do you know?” Jermaine interrupted.

Suddenly, a small wooden cart attached to two large copper wheels rolled between the boys, rattling as it moved over stones. A short, plump woman with wavy salt-and-pepper hair and coconut-brown skin held up a shiny copper teapot. Her white dress was stained with tea spots and flecks of spices. She leaned over and caressed the calf’s silky head and looked at the boys. “Care for some tea?” she asked in Hindi. “The ginger is from my farm.”

“No. Thanks!” the boys said in unison. The tea merchant pulled out two copper mugs from the side of the cart and cleared her throat. The boys declined her offer again. Before they could continue their bickering, the woman grabbed Rashid’s hand and pushed a cold mug into his palm. “Melt away whatever ails you with tea made from love.” Rashid’s chest rose and tears collected under his eyes. He clenched his jaw and held his breath.
“Anger eats away at love,” she said and raised the teapot. “Melt away whatever ails you.”

Rashid groaned. Then, he looked away when a tear rolled down his cheek. Jermaine abandoned his frown and reached for Rashid’s shoulder, but the tea merchant used her free hand to push a mug into his palm. “Melt away whatever ails you,” she said to Jermaine in Hindi.
“What did she say?” Jermaine asked. “Hey—man—I didn’t mean to make you cry.”

The tea merchant tilted the teapot’s spout down over Rashid’s cup. From it, creamy brown tea descended and warmed his hand. Wisps of steam rose from the mug and tickled Rashid’s oily nose—and sweet cardamom and zesty ginger made his mouth water.

When Rashid’s exhale finally came, he coughed before he sniffled. “She said that we shouldn’t be angry at each other.” Jermaine smiled as his mug became warm. “She’s not wrong.” Rashid used his free hand to pluck a silver coin from his pocket. The merchant placed her palm over Rashid’s hand and squeezed. “Love gives.”

Jermaine smiled and Rashid’s breath slowed. Just then, a mother wearing a sleeveless dress made of forest-green cotton approached the tea cart with eight chatty children and a big burlap bag in her right hand. Rashid shifted his gaze to the family, offered the tea merchant the coin, and smiled. “Love gives.” The tea merchant beamed. She nodded before taking the silver coin.

The children pulled at their mother’s dress as she searched her purple satchel for money, but the tea merchant told her the tea was paid for by the two boys. The mother’s amber eyes moved to the boys; Rashid blew into his mug and Jermaine sniffed the tea with a smile. The tea merchant handed miniature copper mugs to the children and instructed them to blow on the tea to cool it down. The mother reached into the burlap sack and pulled out two garlands strung with bright orange marigolds and fragrant jasmine flowers. Rashid and Jermaine were caught off guard when the mother appeared. Rashid’s spine stiffened when she placed a garland around his neck. “No, no, no, no! I’m not a god!” Rashid confessed as the mother put one on Jermaine.
“Thank you!” the mother said in English and returned to her children.

Rashid and Jermaine turned to face each other and gaped. “I thought they put these on statues of the gods?” Jermaine asked. “Do you put these on people?” Rashid took a sip of his tea and felt the warmth slide down his throat. “On statues of gods or around photos of the dead.”
“Well, I know which one I’d rather pick,” Jermaine admitted. “I ain’t ready to die!”
After finishing their tea, the boys handed the mugs to the tea merchant.
“Until we meet again,” she said to Rashid in Hindi.
Jermaine blinked.  “What did she say?”
“That you drink tea faster than a horse drinks water after running for miles!”

“You liar!” Jermaine exclaimed. The cackling boys ventured through the marketplace with the calf: Jermaine’s jaw fell when he saw a shop with a big door made of swords, the calf tried taking more fruit from wooden carts, and Rashid peered into shops lining both sides of the road. Little black birds chirped and picked at scraps of apple and pear near a dress shop. Copper windchimes dangled and chanted from a metalsmith’s rusty roofs. Pure sandalwood incense flowed out from the doorway of a black shop and curled into loose spirals. Jermaine peered into a dark alleyway and wrinkled his nose at a foul stench. “What the hell is in there?”

Rashid pinched the back of the damp shirt clinging to his back and pulled it off his sticky skin. He squinted, his tongue poking out from between his lips. “It’s strictly forbidden to eat animals in Rishikesh—so—um—it must be some kind of industrial business.”

The boys heard splashing, followed by a hooded figure. Their cloak was as dark as soot and their scarlet boots were crusted with thick mud. When they turned to step onto the main road, Rashid saw a sparkly black whip coiled like a serpent at their side. The handle was made of opal and the tip of the whip was sharp as a dagger. A chill ran down Rashid’s spine. “Let’s hurry!”
“Bro. Your mom won’t be mad if we’re a little late—” Jermaine tried to say.
“My mom is not the same in India. She will kill us before death reaches us.”
Jermaine shook his head and chuckled. “Rashid. Your mom is really cool.”
“You don’t know her like I do!” Rashid growled. “She’s nice to you because—”
“Because my family died in a fire?” Jermaine interrupted coldly.

Rashid groaned and pulled his shoulders away from his ears. “Your mom and dad loved you and constantly rooted for you! If you grew up as me you’d know that my mom has this version of me in her head that doesn’t exist. Sonam does whatever she can to appease her. Priya does whatever she wants because she’s just as headstrong as my mom.”
“What about your brother?” Jermaine asked. “And you?”
Rashid groaned and wiped his oily forehead with the back of his hand.
“I don’t think your mother can control Sahil.”
“Nothing could control that boy,” Rashid replied. “He’s like lightning in a bottle.”

“Yeah—and he’s scary good at fighting,” Jermaine admitted. “But what about you?” 
     “I don’t suck up to my mom like Sonam does. And I don’t challenge her like Priya.”
“Then what do you do?”  Jermaine asked and raised a brow.
“I don’t know. I guess I just stay out of her way.”
“Why?”
“Because—”
“Because you’re afraid of standing up for yourself?” Jermaine cut him off.
“No. I just don’t know if there’s anything I can do,” Rashid sighed. “She loves Sonam and Sahil way more than me. She always blows up on me after she gets into fights with Priya.”
“She does love you.” Jermaine said slowly. “But you have to find your voice, man.”

The calf pushed its head against Rashid’s leg and bellowed. Jermaine put an arm around his best friend and sniffled. “I love you, man,” Jermaine said. “You’re my brother from another mother who also loves you.”
Rashid freed himself from Jermaine’s embrace and glared. “Love doesn’t make you sad. Love doesn’t make you feel small. Love doesn't make you feel awful.”

Jermaine winced and pressed his lips together as Rashid pulled his curls. “Love doesn’t punch your stomach with words. Love doesn’t claw your chest.” The calf whined. “Listen, man—” Jermaine tried to say. “No! You listen! You don’t know what it’s like to be a freak of nature!” Rashid stomped the ground, pulled his curls harder. Tears rolled down his cheeks.

“You not feeling safe enough to go into old places or touch old things doesn’t make you a freak, man. I hate hearing people chew! Listen, man. I don’t know what—” Jermaine started to say. “You don’t know what it’s like to be me. You don’t know what it’s like to feel like a freak all of the time. You don’t know what it’s like to feel like you were born in the wrong family. You don’t know what it’s like to hear your own mother call your dream job a waste of intelligence and money. You don’t know what it’s like to have anxiety attacks every time your family fights at the dinner table. You don’t know what it’s like to love someone your parents would never approve of. You don’t know what it’s like having a wicked witch as a sister!”
“Sonam isn’t that bad,” Jermaine said. “She has issues. She’s insecure.”
“ I—hate—her!” Rashid growled, brows lowering. “I cannot wait to move out!”
“You don’t really mean that, man,” Jermaine said. “She doesn’t mean—”
“I do! I hate being in this family! I’d rather be anyone else but me!” Rashid exclaimed.
He dropped his arms and uncurled his fingers; strands of his hair fell to the ground.
